news.title: Could the World Cup be back in the US as soon as 2038? Absolutely
news.lede: Explosion in ticketing and hospitality revenue goes along with a Fifa desire to recoup lost TV income in future deals. As is often the case, it has been left to Donald Trump to say the quiet bit out loud. “We have to do this again and we have to do it while I’m around, you hear that Gianni?” the US president told Fox News in an interview broadcast in the buildup to the World Cup final.

news.title: Was this actually a great World Cup? Here’s my honest verdict
news.lede: At the end of a World Cup of immense scale, chief football writer Miguel Delaney shares some personal reflections on the tournament and his experiences in the host cities in his latest Inside Football newsletter. A bit like that final Spanish goal, or maybe, more fittingly, the Argentine aggression, you knew it was coming. After Gianni Infantino had made the customary claim that this was the best World Cup ever – nay, most spectacular – Donald Trump of course went even bigger. The US president said it was “one of the greatest events, of any kind, ever held.”

news.title: ‘Reclaim football’: Sepp Blatter joins La Liga chief in call for Gianni Infantino to resign
news.lede: Blatter attacks successor as Fifa president. Javier Tebas says system is ‘rotten at its core’. The Fifa president, Gianni Infantino, has come in for heavy criticism from his predecessor, Sepp Blatter, and the La Liga president, Javier Tebas, with the pair united in the belief that he should stand down. As Spain celebrated their World Cup win on Sunday, Tebas accused Infantino of “destroying the football industry” by prioritising the World Cup over national competitions.

news.title: Fifa launches probe into Argentina's post-World Cup final chaos
news.lede: Fifa has launched a disciplinary investigation into Argentina following chaotic scenes after the final whistle of the 2026 World Cup final, with players, coaching staff and the Argentine Football Association (AFA) potentially facing sanctions. World football's governing body confirmed it has appointed a disciplinary and ethics prosecutor to examine the incidents that erupted after Spain's 1-0 extra-time victory over Argentina at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey on Sunday (19 July), reports BBC. The investigation could lead to suspensions for players or officials found guilty of misconduct, while the AFA could also be fined. The disciplinary process comes as Argentina is already facing potential punishment over a separate incident in which players displayed a banner supporting the country's claim over the Falkland Islands after their semi-final victory against England.

news.title: 2018 World Cup winner Steven Nzonzi set to join Dunkerque
news.lede: According to a report from L’Équipe, Steven Nzonzi (37) has agreed a contract with Ligue 2 side USL Dunkerque. The length of the contract has not yet been disclosed, with the midfielder a free agent following the expiration of his deal at Stoke City this summer. Nzonzi made thirty appearances for Stoke in the Championship last season, and a further appearance off the bench in the FA Cup defeat to Fulham FC (2-1). However, at 37, the Frenchman’s contract was not extended, and he was allowed to depart on a free transfer.

news.title: Five players poised to make big transfers after the World Cup
news.lede: Besides the already established football stars who were expected to deliver great performances in the World Cup, the tournament also gave some other players the opportunity to break through and showcase their talents in the world’s greatest stage, getting the attention of European powerhouses. With the tournament already over, clubs will now focus their attention on bolstering their squads during the current transfer window in anticipation to the start of the domestic leagues. Here are five players expected to get big transfers thanks to their World Cup performances. Ayyoub Bouaddi (Morocco) The 18-year-old midfielder ended up his World Cup performance with an astounding 90% of his passes completed according to Sofascore.

news.title: What made the 2026 FIFA World Cup so special?
news.lede: The pinnacle of football rose up from the fiery dunes of Qatar and flew down to North America, born again like a phoenix that comes alive every four years. The FIFA World Cup, the biggest spectacle of the planet unwrapped nearly every emotion that can be experienced through the medium of sport in another edition. Here are the moments that made this one special. Blockbusters and nail-biters [Image: MEXICO CITY, MEXICO - JUNE 11: Raul Jimenez #9 of Mexico celebrates scoring his team’s second goal during the FIFA World Cup 2026 Group A match between Mexico and South Africa at Mexico City Stadium on June 11, 2026 in Mexico City, Mexico.

news.title: Gaza celebrates World Cup victory of Spanish team they took into their hearts
news.lede: People gather at cafes and outdoor screenings to support team after Spain’s backing for Palestinian rights. The Spanish football team’s World Cup win has been celebrated in the streets of Gaza, where Spain’s support for Palestinian rights and Israel’s backing for the Argentinian team turned the competition into more than a football showdown. Gathered at cafes and outdoor screenings, Palestinians defied the risk of Israeli attacks to cheer on a team they had taken to their hearts. Many waving Spanish flags paid tribute to teenage star Lamine Yamal, who brought a Palestinian flag to celebrations for Barcelona’s La Liga win in May.
